coNcLUsroNs
1. Councilmember Penna nay participate in devising a
mitigation program to deal with the identified unreinfoiced
masonry buildings unless the decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeably material financial effect on his financial interests.
2. 
Councilmember Penna may participate in determining if a
seisnic safety proqram should be applied city wide to all
buildings, and participate in devising and adoptinq such a program
unLess the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and
materi.al financial effect on his financial interests, and such
effect is distingruishable froro the effect on the public generally.
FACTS
state 1a!, relating to seismic safety required cities to
identify unreinforced masonry buildinqs prior to January 1, 1990.
The city of South San rrancisco (the trcityn) has identified around
25 privately or.rned unreinforced masonry buildingrs in roughly a
three block downtown area. state law further reguires the city to
adopt a nitigation program. The tlr;le of prograu, however, is left
to the discretion of the city and nay be as simple as notifying
each building owner that the buildinq is potentially hazardous, or
as complex as reguiring an engineering analysis and retrofitting
the building to conforn to current seismic requirements.
The city is in the process of formulating a seismic prograru.
city staff will then present the options to the council at a study
session. Following this study session, it is likely that an
ordinance will be drafted and presented to the city council for
formal action.
Counciluenber Penna is a real estate broker. He is a
property manager for one of the buildings on the list of 25
buildings developed by the city. 
He is a property Danager for
other buildings located in the city, 
In addition, Councilmember
Penna assists olrners of buildings in the city in locatingr tenants
for their buildings. 
The councilmember may not have an on-going
business relationship with some of the owners of such buildings,
such as being designated a listing aqent for the building. 
one of
such buildings is also included in the list of 25 buildings
identified by the city. 
In addition, presumably, councilmember
Penna has acted as a broker in the sale and purchase of
properties.
Due to the recent earthguake in that area, the city council
is considering whether a seismic safety program should be adopted
for alL buildings rather than the unreinforced masonry buildings
identified by the city pursuant to state 1al,r.


Our File No. I-9 0-03 2
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
from making,
participating in, or using his official 
position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest. 
An official 
has a f i.nancial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision wi1]
have a material financial effect, distingnrishable from its effect
on the public Aenerally, on the official 
or a member of his
i'nmediate fanilyr s or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or Dore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore.
(c) Any source of incoDe, other than gifts and
other than Loans by a coromercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggrregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value prowided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of manageuent.
(e) Any donor of, or any internediary or agent
for a donor of, a gift or qifts agtgrregating two
hundred fifty 
dollars ($25o) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official 
within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103 (a) - (e).
Councilnember Penna is a public official. 
(Section 82048.)
He has an interest, presumably worth more than $1,000 in the
business entity, Penna Realty, in which he holds a position of
management. (Section 87103(a), (d).) 
The busj.ness is also a
source of incorne to hin of nore than $250 in the preceding 12
3 An official,s 
" immediate fanilytr includes his spouse and
dependent children. 
(Section 82029.)


Our File No. I-9 0-03 2
Paqe 4
months. This discussion assumes that the owners of the buildings,
which councilmember Penna manages, provide incorne to hirn of Dore
than $250 in the 12 months prior to the decision. 
(Section
82030.) They are, therefore, sources of income to hin within the
meaning of subdivision (c) of Section 87103.4
If Councilmember Penna received a commission by virtue of
being a broker in the sale and purchase of properties, that
comnission may also create disgualifying financial interests.
Regnrlation 18704.3 (copy enclosed) Provides that under such
circumstances the following are deemed to be sources of income to
hin within the meaning of subdivision (c) of section 87103:
(A) The person the broker represents in the
transaction;
(B) If the broker receives a conmission from a
transaction conducted by an aqent working under the
broker's auspices, the Person represented by the
agent;
(c) Any brokerage business entity through
which the broker conducts business; and
(D) Any person who receives a finder's or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who makes a
referral pursuant to a contract with the broker.
Regulation La704.3 (c) (2) (A) ,
(B), (c) and (D).
If Mr. Penna is acting as a broker for the saLe, purchase, or
lease of some property, he nay in certain circumstances be the
recipient of t'promisedrr income within the meaning of subdivision
(c) of Section 87103. Conmission income is deemed rrpromisedrl
incoue to a real estate broker when the sale is pendinq (i.e., the
sale is in escrow). The fact that Councj.lmeraber Penna has a
listing which could generate commission i-ncome does not mean that
the iniorne is "promised.'r (Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-82-192,
copy enclosed. )
Counc j.lnerober Penna rnust disgualify hinself frorn
participating in any decision which wj-1l have a reasonably
4 Presumably, councilnember Penna owns
interest in the business Penna Realty.
income includes a pro-rata share of the
Thus, sources of income to Penna Realty
share to councilmember Penna of $250 or
12 months are deerned sources of income
wj-thin the meaning of Section 87103 (c) '
of income may constitute disqualifying
a greater than 1o-Percent
Councilrnember Penna' s
income of Penna Realty.
which provide a Pro-rata
more in the preceding
to councilmember Penna
AccordinglY, such sources
financial interests.


Our File No. I-9 0-03 2
Page 5
foreseeable and rnaterial financial effect, distinguishable from
the effect on the public qenerallv, on himself, his business Penna
Rea1ty, or from the sources of income to hin described above.
(Section 871o3. )
Foreseeabi l itv
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeably if there
is a substantial likelihood that they wiLl occur. To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision nust be a more than a nere
possibilityi 
however certainty is not required. 
(Downev cares v.
Dovrnev Communitv Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal. APp. 3d 983,
9A9-99Li Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 CaI. App. 3d 817, 822i In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198 (copy enclosed) . ) The Act seeks to
prevent more than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to
prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest.
(witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)
The nitigation plan that roay be adopted by the city nay
entail expenditures to confora a building to any earthquake safety
nitigation measures adopted. Therefore, it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision to adopt a nitiqation plan will have
an econouic effect on the orrners of the identified unreinforced
masonry buildings - some of whom are sources of incoDe to
Counciluember Penna.
It is unclear whether there is a substantial likelihood that
the adoption of mitigation measures, either for the identified
unreinforced masonry buildings, or for all buildings i.n the city,
will have an econonj.c effect on Penna Realty. If, as property
manager for sone of the buildings in the city, Penna Realty would
be called upon to manage the improveDents Dandated by the
uitigati-on rueasures adopted by the city council, there certain)-y
would be a substantial likelihood that the adoption of uritigration
measures will have an economic effect on Penna Realty. fn
addition, if Penna Realty has been locating and providing tenants
for some of the buildings on an exclusi.ve basis, even though no
fornaL written agreements exist, delays resulting fron makinq the
improvements mandated by the roitigation measures may also have a
reasonably foreseeable economic effect on Penna Realty. You and
councilmember Penna, r^rith the detailed facts and circumstances
available to you in individual- cases, are better able to determine
the foreseeability of an econornic effect on Penna Realty.
t{aterialitv
Regulation 18702 sets forth the gnridelines for deternining
whether an official's 
financial interest in a decision is
rrmateriallyrr affected as reguired by section 871o3. If the
official,s 
financial interest is directlv involved in the
decisj,on, then Regulation 18702.1 (copy encl,osed) applies to
determine nateriality. 
Thus, for example, if Penna Rea1ty is
directlv involved in the decision before the city council, the
effect of the decision would be deemed rnaterial. on the other


Our File No. I-9 0-03 2
Page 6
hand, if the official,s 
financial interest is indirectlv affected
by the decision, then Regrulations 18702.2 to fa7!2.6 (copies
enclosed) would apply to determine whether the effect of the
decision is material.
In the circumstances described, Penna ReaLty will be
indirectly affected by the decision to adopt the proposed
miti<ration neasures. The effect of the decision will be deemed
nateiiar if :5
(1) The decision wilL result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,00o or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existinq expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in an increase
- or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
Regrulation L87O2.2 (gl .
Regnrlation L8702.2 may similarly be applied to business
entities which are sources of income to Councilmember Penna to
deteroine rrhether the decision rrill have a uateri.al financial
effect on such business entities. 
Regulation la702.6 would apply
to an individual who is a source of income to Councilmember Penna
to determine whether the effect of the decision is material.
PubIic cenerallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision is
material, disqualification is required only if the effect is
distingruishable from the effect on the public generally. 
(Section
87103. ) For the city, the public consists of all residents of the
city. 
Thus disqualification is required unless the decision will
affect Councilmember Penna's financial interest in substantially
the same rDanner as it will affect all rnenbers of the public or a
significant segment of the public. 
(Regrulation 18703, coPy
enclosed).
The ci.ty has identified 25 privately owned unreinforced
masonry buildings in roughly a three-block area downtown. Thus a
decision to adopt nitigation neasures for the identified
5 rhi= assunes that Penna Realty is a business entity of the
financial size covered by subdivision (g), of Regulation 78702.2
which applies to the smallest business entity. 
If Penna Realty,s
financial size is different, one of the other subdivisions of
Regulation 18702.2 may be applicable.


Our File No. I-9 0-03 2
Page 7
unreinforced masonry buildings will affect only these 25
buildings. 
The property owners of these building cannot be deemed
to be a significant segTment of the public. 
Therefore, the effect
on Councilmember Penna's financial interests is distinquishabte
frorn the effect on the public generally. Accordingrly,
councilrnember Penna must disgualify hinself from participating in
the decision to adopt nitigation measures for such unreinforced
masonry buildings if the effect of the decision on his financial
interests is naterial..
The decisj.on to adopt nitigation measures for all buildings
in the city rnay be sirnilarly analyzed to determine lrhether the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public qenerally.
If, for exanple, the nitigation alternatives proposed by staff
exclude all singrle farnily residences, a large segment of the
city's residents will be excluded from the rnitigation program. In
that case, the number of residents affected by the proposed
roitigation measures may not constitute a significant seqment of
the public within the meaning of Regmlation 18703. In addition,
if the effect of the decision on councilneBber Penna's financial
interest is deemed material, and if the financial interest is a
source of income owning a large number of properties in the city,
it L/ould be difficult 
to clain that such source of income would be
affected in substantially the same Danner as a significant segrment
of the public. 
It is unlikely that a significant seqment of the
residenls of the city would own a large number of properties in
the city. 
Thus, you have to determine on a case-by-case basis
whether the reasonably foreseeable material financiaL effect on a
financial interest is distingnrishable from the effect on the
public generally.6
I trust this letter provides you with the gnridance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
Sincerely,
Kathrlm E. Donovan
General Counsel
Counsel, Legal Division
KED /JSA/ aa
Enc Iosures
6 To aid you in naking such a deternination, I have enclosed
copies of conmission opinions and advice letters which have
addressed the issue. Enclosed are: fn re ovren (7976) 2 FPPC ops.
77i fn re Lecran (1985) 9 FPPC ops. 1i In re Ferraro (1978) FPPC
Ops. 62; West Advice Letter, No. A-89-243; Scher Advice Letter,
No. A-88-479; Flvnn Advice Letter, No. f-88-430.

sections.question: Whether Councilmember Penna may participate in decisions regarding a seismic safety mitigation program for unreinforced masonry buildings and a potential city-wide seismic safety program, given his interests as a real estate broker and property manager.
sections.conclusion: 1. Councilmember Penna may participate in devising a mitigation program to deal with the identified unreinforced masonry buildings unless the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeably material financial effect on his financial interests. 2. Councilmember Penna may participate in determining if a seismic safety program should be applied city wide to all buildings, and participate in devising and adopting such a program unless the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on his financial interests, and such effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

If Penna Realty,s
financial size is different, one of the other subdivisions of
Regulation 18702.2 may be applicable.


Our File No. I-9 0-03 2
unreinforced masonry buildings will affect only these 25
buildings. 
The property owners of these building cannot be deemed
to be a significant segTment of the public. 
Therefore, the effect
on Councilmember Penna's financial interests is distinquishabte
frorn the effect on the public generally. Accordingrly,
councilrnember Penna must disgualify hinself from participating in
the decision to adopt nitigation measures for such unreinforced
masonry buildings if the effect of the decision on his financial
interests is naterial..
The decisj.on to adopt nitigation measures for all buildings
in the city rnay be sirnilarly analyzed to determine lrhether the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public qenerally.
If, for exanple, the nitigation alternatives proposed by staff
exclude all singrle farnily residences, a large segment of the
city's residents will be excluded from the rnitigation program. In
that case, the number of residents affected by the proposed
roitigation measures may not constitute a significant seqment of
the public within the meaning of Regmlation 18703. In addition,
if the effect of the decision on councilneBber Penna's financial
interest is deemed material, and if the financial interest is a
source of income owning a large number of properties in the city,
it L/ould be difficult 
to clain that such source of income would be
affected in substantially the same Danner as a significant segrment
of the public. 
It is unlikely that a significant seqment of the
residenls of the city would own a large number of properties in
the city. 
Thus, you have to determine on a case-by-case basis
whether the reasonably foreseeable material financiaL effect on a
financial interest is distingnrishable from the effect on the
public generally.6
I trust this letter provides you with the gnridance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Councilmember Penna, a real estate broker and property manager with financial interests in buildings affected by proposed seismic safety programs, participate in city council decisions regarding mitigation measures for unreinforced masonry buildings and potential city-wide seismic safety programs?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Councilmember Penna may participate in seismic safety decisions unless those decisions have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on his interests that is distinguishable from effects on the public generally. Disqualification depends on whether Penna Realty would directly manage improvements or receive other income effects meeting the materiality thresholds defined in the regulations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains OCR errors and illegible sections in header and regulatory references. Question and Conclusion sections are clearly identified. Analysis section is extensive but somewhat truncated in provided text. Facts section clearly establishes Councilmember Penna's potential conflicts of interest.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Councilmember Penna may participate in decisions regarding a seismic safety mitigation program for unreinforced masonry buildings and a potential city-wide seismic safety program, given his interests as a real estate broker and property manager.

CONCLUSION: 1. Councilmember Penna may participate in devising a mitigation program to deal with the identified unreinforced masonry buildings unless the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeably material financial effect on his financial interests. 2. Councilmember Penna may participate in determining if a seismic safety program should be applied city wide to all buildings, and participate in devising and adopting such a program unless the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on his financial interests, and such effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

FACTS state 1a!, relating to seismic safety required cities to identify unreinforced masonry buildinqs prior to January 1, 1990. The city of South San rrancisco (the trcityn) has identified around 25 privately or.rned unreinforced masonry buildingrs in roughly a three block downtown area. state law further reguires the city to adopt a nitigation program. The tlr;le of prograu, however, is left to the discretion of the city and nay be as simple as notifying each building owner that the buildinq is potentially hazardous, or as complex as reguiring an engineering analysis and retrofitting the building to conforn to current seismic requirements. The city is in the process of formulating a seismic prograru. city staff will then present the options to the council at a study session. Following this study session, it is likely that an ordinance will be drafted and presented to the city council for formal action. Counciluenber Penna is a real estate broker. He is a property manager for one of the buildings on the list of 25 buildings developed by the city. He is a property Danager for other buildings located in the city, In addition, Councilmember Penna assists olrners of buildings in the city in locatingr tenants for their buildings. The councilmember may not have an on-going business relationship with some of the owners of such buildings, such as being designated a listing aqent for the building. one of such buildings is also included in the
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter analyzing whether Councilmember Penna may participate in city council decisions regarding seismic safety mitigation programs for unreinforced masonry buildings, given his business interests as a real estate broker and property manager. The FPPC concludes participation is permitted unless the decisions have reasonably foreseeable and material financial effects distinguishable from public effects.
